replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Do you think that polytheists have a certain blindness to demonoltary? I really hate asking this question -- I'm a serious devotional polytheist! -- but reading the discourse on the matter, it seems that the devotional polytheist turns a blind eye to some degree of demon-worship. Perhaps the community is more amenable to the sort of argument that goes "Christians said *your* god was a demon, and so why should you care that I worship Satan." or what have you.

Indeed, reading prominent devotional polytheist blogs I don't think I've seen much about demons or other malicious beings, or the spiritual discernment needed to make sure one is not dealing with one. This seems like a serious oversight.

Part of the issue is that -- at least in the Greek myths I've studied -- there are no coherent concept of demons. Indeed the word demon comes, of course, from δαίμων which simply meant something akin to spirit. The ancient Greeks had the concept of κακοδαίμων, or evil spirits. That said, studying Hesiod's Theogony I fail to see the sort of coherence one gets with Christianity's taxonomic study of the hierarchy of evil beings.

In the War of the Titans the Titans are confined to a place "beyond gloomy Chaos" which sounds like Hell, but again there is a lack of coherence. Iris, a Titaness, is mentioned in a nearby passage as the messenger of the gods! Likewise many other Titans who don't get condemned to misty Tartarus!

Pausanius makes it very clear in his books that there were some seriously creepy cults in ancient Greece -- there was one that demanded human blood. the Greeks would whip boys and the blood would fall on the statue and this was their best work around. Still, to my mind, I wondered why they didn't cast some divinations about asking Apollon to shut the whole bloodthirsty temple down!

For these reasons I wonder if reconstructionists especially might lack some of the psychic immunization needed for dealing with demons rather than δαίμωνές. One of the benefits of the radical dualism of the Magian worldview is that it's not afraid to call an evil spirit an evil spirit. Of course, one can go way too far in this direction. That said, I tend to agree with Spengler that late period Neoplatonism has more than a touch of the Magian...

I'm curious your thoughts on this, JMG and other readers. While this is certainly a personally painful topic, I think that some good can come of conversation, and I also acknowledge that I may be missing crucial points and full of misunderstanding.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's a complicated issue. Classical Greeks and Romans knew perfectly well that there were spiritual beings it was unhealthy to worship -- that's why both Athens and Rome required anybody who wanted to introduce the worship of a new deity to get approval from the legislature first. The creepier cults endured because they were ancient, their effects were well known, and whatever benefits the gods gave as a result of them were considered worth having. I'm sure that the Greeks did do divinations, and were told to keep doing the old rites!

As for modern polytheists, yeah, there's a fair amount of live and let live, and on the whole that's a good thing -- but it does have drawbacks.

replies.title: demons and bad stuff in general
replies.content:
First a reply re Lucifer in particular. There have been a number of people and groups in the past decades claiming to be sort of Luciferian Gnostics--i.e. they believe that Lucifer, in 'tempting' Adam and Eve to partake of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil were doing humanity a favor--making us, as the Bible itself warns, "like gods" and that the God of the Old Testament was trying to keep humanity enslaved and unable to reach true gnosis. As to whether the person on the board mentioned is a Luciferian of this type, too little information.

As to apparent abandonment of 'an it harm none, etc.' some people have convinced themselves that Gardner invented the Threefold law as a way to seem harmless to the Christians, along with the rules against taking money for magic, against using magic to drive a hard bargain, etc. He is seen as deliberately distancing himself and the religion he was promoting from the cunning woman who would sell love spells, curses, etc. along with more benign activities like locating lost goods or selling healing herbs. So, many of the people with this opinion have taken to calling themselves Witches rather than Wiccans or distinguishing between the various Gardnarian type lineages and what they call Traditional Witchcraft. Some declare that their witchcraft is a technology, not a religion. Some people have crossed over from one group to the other, but many are newcomers making their own version of witchcraft and disdaining the path of initiation, oaths and Craft Laws. One cannot rule out the possibility that some of these people sought initiation into British Traditional Witchcraft but were refused as not being willing to do the work or as not being proper persons.

I think some regard traditional admonitions against certain kinds of magic as being the equivalent of Queensbury rules of boxing, all very well for the gentlefolk doing it as a sport, but not much use in a back alley fight for survival. In this current political climate they have cast themselves as the defenders of the downtrodden. I would say this applies to magical workers on both sides--the alt-right Pepe the frog defenders of Trump are as sure that they are defending Western civilization as the left wing creators of spells against the current administration are.

replies.title: murk surrounding Lucifer
replies.content:
Lucifer's name means "Bearer of Light" in Latin.

I'm not well versed in Christian angelology and demonology, but the little I have read about Lucifer indicates that his only or his primary sin is disobedience, or perhaps setting himself up as a rival to God's authority. That makes him attractive to anyone who is rebellious.

In European culture, the witch (speaking here only of mortal human beings who practice witchcraft) is someone who practices her craft without a license, so to speak, from any religious or secular authority. This does not necessarily imply that she is opposed to or in revolt against society's authority figures, only that she is unwilling to come under their control.

When you combine the anticlericalism of the Enlightenment with the Romantic movement of the nineteenth century, a lot of people are prompted to go looking for religion and spirituality in religions that the Church has rejected or opposed. The ones nearest to hand are Classical Paganism, pre-Christian European religions, and fallen angels.

In 1862, historian Jules Michelet wrote La Sorciere (English title Satanism and Witchcraft), which presents the witches' sabbats described by the witch hunters of earlier centuries as a kind of symbolic peasant rebellion, invoking Satan as liberator. This idea of witches as workers of magic on behalf of the poor and oppressed against the church and nobility appealed to some of the intelligentsia. In the late nineteenth century, the English folklorist Charles Godfrey Leland spent time in Italy looking for pagan survivals in northern Italian folklore. As he grew older, he did less of his own fieldwork and depended more on an informant who brought him charms, and a Vangelo that begins,

"This is the Gospel of the Witches: Diana greatly loved her brother Lucifer, the god of the Sun and of the Moon, the god of Light (Splendor), who was so proud of his beauty, and who for his pride was driven from Paradise.

Diana had by her brother a daughter, to whom they gave the name of Aradia [i.e. Herodias].

In those days there were on earth many rich and many poor.

The rich made slaves of all the poor. . . "

The above is the opening of Aradia or the Gospel of the Witches, a book which had a lasting influence on people's ideas about medieval and early modern European witchcraft. Passages from Aradia (not the ones quoted above) are incorporated in the most widely known liturgical work of twentieth century Wicca, The Charge of the Goddess.

I have a fairly busy sixth house, which seems to relate a lot to illnesses. Since these are merely probabilities, I want to reduce my chance of getting a major illness. As such, I plan to put a bit of work into getting healthy to avoid the worst outcomes. Since the energies flow downwards, I think my physical self won’t be healthy unless the higher levels are as well, so I’m trying to figure out a plan to work on the health for all levels of my being.
Starting at the top, I have no idea how to work on the spiritual. I’m not even sure if I’ve ever experienced it at all, so I’m not sure if it makes sense to try to work on it. I’ve also seen that human beings stop at the mental sheath, and so I don’t know if there’s anything I can do to work on health this high up.
The mental body seems simple: a habit of discursive meditation would feed it and strengthen it, and otherwise I can’t think of anything else which it needs.
The astral body will benefit from a banishing ritual, and I can exercise it through scrying. I may be off the mark here, but I suspect that deep sleep is rest for the astral body, and dreaming serves a purpose as well: so getting plenty of sleep is a must.
The etheric body has a few needs as well: sleep, again; actual food and plastic food-like substances; water (given water picks up etheric energies, I’d imagine pure water would be healthy for the etheric body); deep breathing; time outside soaking energies; and exercise.
On the physical level, sleep (again), plenty of food, water, breathing, and exercise
Therefore, I figure that the following practices will help with my health, at all levels: meditation, scrying, making sure to get plenty of sleep, a banishing ritual, scrying weekly, the breathing and drinking exercies from the Order of Essenes course, making real food, playing an instrument to exercise my etheric body, at least fifteen minutes outside a week, and some training in karate to get a good physical work out.
How do these practices sound for maintaining and improving health?
Are there other practices which you think I missed which would help with health?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, you can't start from the top, since humans haven't yet developed the necessary subtle bodies to operate on those planes. The mental plane is as high as you can go, since you're developing a mental body at this phase of your evolution; you can orient it toward the spiritual levels by meditation and prayer, which is useful.

Second, it's been my experience that the worst thing you can do for your health is stress out about it and spend lots of time trying to control it. Worrying less is one of the best things you can do for health; it also helps to work on listening to your bodies (on all levels) and encouraging them to tell you what they need, rather than deciding that you're going to do this and that and the other for (or to) them.

Third, the sixth house also has a lot to do with employment -- both being an employee and having employees -- adn with pets and small animals. What's the sign on the cusp of your sixth, and how and where is the planet that rules that sign placed in your chart? That will tell you how those various planets in your sixth tend to express themselves.

replies.title: Re: Several questions (saved up)
replies.content:
Not a professional musician by any means, but I played the French horn seriously for seven years and had prior experience with the cello (thank you, public school music program!) and the inner dimension of an instrument is revealed by playing it. A lot. Practicing. Then playing with other people. Different kinds of music (say, marching band, orchestral, and wood wind quintets.) Feeling it. Knowing the instrument, the way you would know a dear friend or lover. An instrument can tell you stories, it has a personality, it appreciates having a name, it creates emotion, it can pull you in different directions.

replies.title: Re: Light and Magic Lanterns
replies.content:
1) Eliphas Levi included a lantern with colored shades in his magical toolkit, though I don't know of anyone who's done it since his time. Colored light was also used by 19th century Rosicrucians for healing and magic, so you might as well experiment.

2) Some forms of illumination are indeed magically better than others. Why do you think mages use candles so consistently? Light from flame strengthens magic, light from the blue end of the electric-light spectrum can interfere with it.

About electric lights: cinematographer John Ott was the inventor of time lapse photography of the sort that shows flowers blooming and other such natural processes. As a result of this work, he studied the effects of various kinds of light on living organisms. He found sunlight to be best. comments.title: Polytheism and monotheism
comments.content:
Hello!

As a polytheist,

(1) would you say that monotheists have been short-changed or misguided in believing that there is only one god?

(2) If so, would that not be unethical of a god (among many!) to pass off as the one and only?

(3) Surely, the Supreme God ought to be quite annoyed if a lesser god would try (succesfully) to pass off at being the one and only?

(4) Or do you think that what are considered as angels and archangels in christianity for instance are gods in someonelse's polytheist religion?

Sorry if my queries are not entirely related to magic!

Many thanks anyway!

replies.title: Re: Polytheism and monotheism
replies.content:
Okay, to answer this it's going to be necessary to get into a little history of religions. Very broadly speaking, there are two categories of religions, which we can call natural religions and prophetic religions. (Prophetic religions like to call this latter category "revealed religions.") A natural religion grows out of the religious experience of a people over time. A prophetic religion is invented by an individual (the prophet) who rejects the religious experience of his people and insists that people should follow his rules instead. Natural religions are pretty much always polytheist. Prophetic religions are more often monotheist, though of course there are exceptions. Clear so far?

My take, for what it's worth, is that prophetic religions are always contaminated by the ego of the prophet, who says in effect, "My religious experience is better than your religious experience, and my god is better than your god." Monotheism is the extreme expression of that ego trip: "There's only one god and he's mine, mine, mine!" It's one of the distinctive features of prophetic religions that they insist that a scripture written by the prophet or his disciples takes precedence over everyone else's ongoing experience of deities. Thus it doesn't matter, to believers in a prophetic religion, what the god does or doesn't say; what matters is what the prophet said about the god. (And of course "Our scripture is better than your scripture" is another dimension of the ego trip.)

The gods of monotheist religions seem to be far more tolerant than most of their worshipers. I know a lot of people, for example, who combine reverence for Christ with reverence for other gods and goddesses, and their experience is that Christ doesn't mind this at all -- however many fits his self-proclaimed spokespersons might throw over such a thing. Thus it seems likely to me that Christ, for example, is one god out of many, who had the misfortune of having some of his followers go zooming off on an ego trip of the sort just described.

replies.title: Re: Polytheism and monotheism
replies.content:
Hmm, some religious movements/sects within broader polytheistic traditions and seem to skirt the line on this. For example, there are sects within Hinduism (I'm thinking Krishna-worshipping Vaishnavas) wherein the teachers and practitioners staunchly insist that their chosen Supreme Lord personality is the Supreme Lord and not the other's sect's choice; in these groups the founding Guru of the lineage in question is treated like a de-facto Prophet, according to the criteria you laid out above. A lot of modern Hindus in general consider the various demigods to be just aspects of whatever Supreme Personality they revere. And of course Hindu offshoots like Buddhism and Sikhism that would fall largely in your prophetic category.

Zoroastrianism is another interesting case too, as as it contains elements of both natural religion (there's a sizable pantheon of Indo-Iranian demigods (Yazatas) representing all the main aspects of nature and cosmology) and an original "prophetic" revelation (which reads more like a collection of Vedic hymns, but I digress...) dictating the core doctrine. Having studied Zoroastrianism quite a bit, I think Ahura Mazda (literally: "Wise Lord" or "Lord Wisdom") would be quite displeased with people using his likeness for sectarian or egoistic purposes, as his main message seems to be that he wants people to be free-thinkers and arrive at religious insights by their own choice, and most importantly seek enlightenment and shun ignorance, in the most universal/general sense possible. My personal hunch is that at sometime during the axial age the original tradition got concretized and corrupted into a dualistic, rules-based religion.

replies.title: Re: Polytheism and monotheism
replies.content: Prophetic religions come in various flavors, and yes, the various Vaisnava sects with their "Supreme Personality of God" -- whichever one it happens to be -- are a typically Hindu variant on prophetic religion. Agreed about Zoroastrianism -- a lot of religions came through the Axial Age very much the worse for wear.

comments.title: Solitude and The Watcher
comments.content:
I have two questions this week that are probably more related than not.

The first: I’ve found myself reflecting lately on the much discussed concept of the spiritual path as “the path of the lonely ones.

Dion Fortune once said “Those whose fate destines them for the inner path are prevented from turning outward and finding their place in life. It is a painful process at the time, but the result is a personality that can be used for the purpose for which it was designed, being free from attachments and inured to solitude. Such, I believe is the law of the higher path.”

When karmic ties of the sort that transcend single lifetimes are brought up its generally with the implication that those entanglements will eventually have to be smoothed out so that those individuals can be free of each other, and most of the authors I’ve encountered tend to be pretty skeptical of the idea of “soul mates.” All this seems to imply that the attainment of Gwynfydd is a completely solitary thing, that the processing that goes on between lives is between us and whatever higher powers take an interest in us and any of the reunions with loved ones so often longed for in popular theology or encountered in various NDEs is actually an encounter with astral dream projections drawn from memory. Do any of the human relationships and bonds we build in this phase of our development carry over in any way into the next one? Or is part of the social aspect of human existence about building those bonds, learning what we can from each other, letting go and moving on towards a solitary destiny never to cross paths again?

The second: (and the above thoughts are probably an example, as was my question last week)... I’ve been practicing divination, meditation and banishing ritual without interruption for about a year now and have had nothing but positive effects from it. However, for about 3 weeks now I have out of nowhere themes from meditations I’ve done or snippets of the pieces of my reading I’ve been keeping in my background consciousness grabbing hold of my mind in grotesque, distorted ways. The afterlife as some sort of solitary cage where your ruach is kept placid by dream projections while its slowly digested over a thousand years by the higher self... or a perception of the higher self as some sort of extra-dimensional alien parasite absorbing my memories and experiences and then moving on to a new host after this personality dies... (those are some of the most obviously distorted examples). Most of the time they go away after some heavy journaling but they keep popping up in various forms. I haven’t dealt with the old issues of boredom, distractedness, or excuses to do anything but the work for months now except very occasionally, but now it’s like... something’s trying to take ideas and images I’d found beautiful and inspiring and make them disturbing. Is this a normal part of the process? My guess is that now that I’m having fewer issues with my body rebelling by way of itching and twitching and with my subconscious rebelling by way of random distracting thoughts, my ego has set himself in place to be the third ruffian on the initiatory path (though that’s a metaphor with implications of its own).

replies.title: Re: Solitude and The Watcher
replies.content: Oh gosh the signature fell off again. -The above comment was from Eric Singletary.

replies.title: Re: Solitude and The Watcher
replies.content:
1) If you have unresolved karma with somebody, you'll meet them in future lives. If you have strong bonds of affection, ditto. It gets awkward if you're on a spiritual path and the other person isn't; on the other hand, if the two of you are on parallel paths, there can be quite a few interactions from life to life. The phrase "The Way of the Lonely Ones" refers to your dealings with people in the mass, not to individuals.

2) You are your higher self. Imagine that you had been stuffed as an infant into a gorilla suit; imagine that the gorilla suit was flexible enough that it stretched as you grew. Imagine that all your life you thought the gorilla's face was your face, the gorilla's fur is your fur, and so on. Now imagine that you've been provided with a set of odd exercises that have the goal of finding the zipper.

Your higher self is the self you've never seen, the face you had -- as the Zen koan puts it -- before your mother and father were born. The gorilla suit is your lower self, the self you think you are. You will be casting off the gorilla suit in due time, but that doesn't mean that you go away and some other being feeds on a gorilla; it means you recognize that what you thought you were is not what you are.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
What are the issues you’re having? That’ll help with pinpointing what needs to be reset. When a computer has a huge host of issues that seem unrelated, that’s usually due to OS corruption though it can be a failing motherboard or hard drive. Also did you assemble the computer on the birthday or did you just start it and set up the OS on that day?

First I’ll check if the hard drive is failing. My go-to software for this is Seagate SeaTools (https://www.seagate.com/support/downloads/seatools/). If it is failing, then you’ll need to replace the hard drive and reinstall the OS.

The best way to clear a computer is on the software level. Computer identity is more heavily tied to software than hardware*. You can assemble and disassemble a computer all day without it noticing. Assuming the hard drive is good, I would wipe the hard drive preferably by setting all the bits to 0. The software I use for that is Darik’s Boot and Nuke (https://sourceforge.net/projects/dban/). It may not work on SSDs however. After that, you can install the OS just like it was a new computer.

While reinstalling the OS, be sure that the computer’s name is different. Nowadays most computers set their name automatically by using the first 6-8 letters of the user name + random letter and numbers. I’ll change the user name when setting up the OS which will change the computer name. Worst case, you can manually change it. I would expect backups to be fine especially if they’re just copied files. I’ll be wary of restoring from an image though.

*- This aspect of modern computing has a huge impact on how many people especially in tech circles view the relationship between body and mind. “The mind is software and the body is hardware” is a common refrain.

comments.title: A question about connections
comments.content:
Dear JMG


I have an unusual problem and would be so glad of your advice.


My father died ten years ago. It's fair to say that I and the rest of the family had a difficult relationship with him. Before he died he became involved with a sect of Japanese Mikkyo (密教) associated with Kukai (空海) cult. Members of my family have recently told me that he agreed to forge some kind of esoteric connection (which was described as some kind of karmic term 'en' 縁) between our family and the cult. To this end he gave the cult the birthdates of all family members. Most worryingly, passports belonging to family members and photographs of my young daughter were stolen by a member of this cult and some allusions were made by my father about the child (his grandaughter) being abducted by the cult in the future.


A psychic has told me that an esoteric contract has indeed been made between my family and the Mikkyo cult. The psychic said that unless that esoteric bond is severed, there are chances that my daughter may be in some danger. However, the psychic did not know how such a bond could be severed.


I am not sure what to make of any of this and I'm sure it all sounds completely bizarre. But my question is, is it likely that such a connection has been made between my family and this cult (seeing as none of us, except my father, agreed to such a connection)? And is there a way to remove such a  connection?

Thanks for any advice

replies.title: Re: A question about connections
replies.content:
No, this doesn't sound bizarre at all. On the one hand, such things happen in Western magic as well as the Japanese variety; on the other, my stepmom is Japanese, and my Japanese stepfamily are all Shingon Buddhists, so I've got some exposure to the ideas and practices you're dealing with.

This is a serious issue, but fortunately there are ways to deal with it successfully. The best way I know of is to invoke an appropriate supernatural power to help you. Since the Mikkyo cult in question is defiling the Dharma by abusing it in this way, the power I'd recommend is Fudo Myo-O. To invoke his protective power, you'll need a Buddhist rosary of 108 beads, and a color printout of the following image, which is Fudo's seed syllable KAN:
[IMAGE:0]
Take the rosary and, while looking at the seed syllable, recite the following, which is Fudo's Mantra of Compassionate Help:
[IMAGE:1]
Noomaku sanmanda bazaradan senda makaroshada sowataya un tarata kanman

Recite it 108 times once a day for 108 days. Do this with the intention of asking Fudo's help to break the connection with the cult. At some point during that time, visit a Buddhist temple where Fudo is enshrined, make an appropriate offering, and pray to Fudo, asking for his help. You'll get it.

comments.title: Rock Shop
comments.content:
Hi,

I had an unusual experience over the weekend that I’m trying to make sense of.

I was on a family vacation at a ‘tourist-trap’ kind of town and saw a rock shop. I thought it would be an opportunity to see if I could find stones mentioned in ENM. My family isn’t up to speed on all of this so I sold a trip to the store as an opportunity to find my 5 year old a ‘pretty rock’.

When I went in the store, it was not a new-age hippie store. It was a collector’s store with thousands of rocks presented matter of factly on shelves. I immediately noticed that I was cold, a feeling, not a nod to the air conditioning. As I walked around the store, my brain shut down. I couldn’t process all the types of rocks and when I read labels, my brain wasn’t connecting with anything else. It was like my database shut down. I just felt a need to get out.

I left without buying anything except a pretty rock for the kid. When we were checking out, the employee, who we hadn't spoken to before, saw my child and offered a ‘quiz’. It’s a tourist town so I thought this might be a standard entertainment thing for all the kids that come in. He had her look at a crystal and notice that as it turned, it could make one line on a piece of paper into two – basic stuff. He asked her some yes/no questions, like “Do you listen to your parents?” and then had her touch the rock to turn it. I got a very bad feeling, like something off was happening, and I immediately, in my mind, shouted “no” at this third eye like I had read here and then imagined a rose boundary between them (a new agey meditation I picked up a decade ago). After that, he seemed distracted and looked around and found another kid to do his routine with. I left feeling very strange about the encounter.

For comparison, about an hour later, we went into a new age, hippie store, with that standard, small selection of healing rocks and crystals and I got no cold or strange feelings like in the other store.

1 – Was there something about the rocks in the rock shops’s inventory that made my brain get crossed? I wish I could give you some names of what was there, but I couldn’t process anything at the time. There were hundreds of rocks from everywhere.

2 – Was this man using a crystal to do some kind of magic on my child and if so, what can I do? When I was praying that night, I got the sense that there was some kind of residual shell, like a turtle shell molded to her as a result of that I prayed for help removing it.
I’m a regular reader, I’ve read the FAQ, I do an SOP 5-6 days a week along with some occasional hoodoo floor washes and prayer. I feel really bad that I walked her into that situation and am wondering what I can do now and what else I can do in the future when strange people put their attention on my kid. In retrospect, I wish I had done something more in the moment, but I'm not sure what.

Thank you for your time.
AL

replies.title: Re: Rock Shop
replies.content:
Hi AL,
my first name is Petra, usually taken to mean "rock." I've been to many rock shops, some gem & mineral conventions, and have dug for petrified wood in Wyoming. I adore rocks, their geological meaning, where and how they occur, how the Earth has created concoctions of minerals likely unique.
And yes, I keep ears wide open to the lore.
What you describe of your sense of the first store strikes me as weird and inappropriate behavior of the salesperson. Am not sure which kind of rock "makes a line"-- do you mean a piece of, say, selenite (calcium carbonate in elongated crystal form) or something carved into a wand?
I do not like that the salesperson went beyond conducting store business.
He should not have held yours or any of yours' interest beyond informing about the product courteously. Rockhound shops are usually all about the beauty & fascination of the rocks themselves.
If in your place, I'd hold the rock you bought under cold running water for a good while. And, I'd put it outside in direct sunlight.
I heard/read somewhere that rocks don't like to move. (Makes sense, doesn't it?)
Maybe those rocks accummulated for profit alone didn't like that....

BTW there are good, serious websites for rock/mineral ID.

replies.content: Optical calcite, aka a sunstone

replies.title: Re: Rock Shop
replies.content:

Cold water is a very traditional means of spiritual purification. Homer has Agamemnon and his warriors bathe in the sea for purification at the beginning of the Iliad; Japanese occultists and holy people call the same practice misogi shuho and do it regularly. I've done it at a Shinto shrine north of Seattle, in a river that flows from a mountain named Glacier Peak -- I'll let you imagine the temperature of the water.

As for a prayer, well, what deities do you pray to and what kind of magic do you practice? Such things aren't one-size-fits-all...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dear Anonymous -

Your post touched on a painful wound of mine -- I went through the same situation in my life, and have asked myself the same questions. So even though I'm a week late to reading this, I will respond.

And I'm going to respond as if I am the person you are talking about --

We had a chance to sort out the karmic issues, isn't that why we were brought together in the first place? The karmic ties were there before we met, no? But instead of resolving them, we simply repeated or strengthened the karma, right? Who rejected love, was it me or you? Who rejected healing, me or you? Who deliberately hurt the other person? Who damaged whom the most? Who lost the most in that spectacular blowup you refer to? I was willing to resolve the issues and work with you -- I was practically obsessed with it! In my view, you are the one who shut me down, shut me out, stabbed me in the back, and in the heart. I became a disgraced exile because of you. And still I dreamed of reconciliation, for a while. Years of strict no contact have finally broken the spell, and my life has recovered, on the outside I'm doing better than ever. On the inside, I wonder when I will finally be healed, or if this pain will be with me for the rest of my life? I have done so many meditations, prayers, cleansing rituals, yoga, journaling, love, forgiveness, you name it, to try to cut these ties and get on with my life. They worked for a while, but the anger always came back. The idea of a future life where justice - or is it revenge? - is finally served to you for what you did to me, is, to be honest, a consoling thought for me. I struggle to let go of anger, to not think of myself as the victim, to see this as a necessary chapter in my spiritual development. But, will anger and love both fade out over time? Or is there something left to deal with in future lives?

I think that it's disingenuous for you to say that you'd "rather sort out these issues now." Yeah, right. We had that chance, and you blew it in a major way. You get the karma you choose, and now you're complaining about it! Nevertheless, your instincts are correct -- better now than next life. The problem is that I now view you as a toxic and untrustworthy person to avoid at all costs. The only way of dealing with the issues would be through a neutral third party, with counseling of some kind. But what is the point of counseling two people who do not want to have anything to do with each other?

And yet -- I'm still somewhat open to this suggestion. I have been actively moving on with my life, but maybe trying to resolve this bad karma would be good for both of us. If you can find a neutral spiritual counselor willing to do this work with us, then have them get in touch with me. I'm not hard to find. But I do not wish to speak with you directly, and I will not meet with you one on one.

P.S. --
Dear Anonymous -- I hope this helps you understand the mind of your karmic friend; and if not, at least - know that you're not alone. I hope you find the liberation you long for.
JMG -- sorry for this long, rather personal post; I will not be offended if you choose not to post it. Thank you for this most excellent forum.

Chef

comments.title: Magic in odd-shaped quarters
comments.content:
So much of magic is oriented towards the 4 quarters. I am in an L-shaped apartment with a SW/NE orientation.

Of course, instead of having N-S-E_W walls, I have corners. With my pocket compass and the floor lam given in the brochure, I worked out that my East corner is in the furthest back corner of the kitchen counters, but at least it's visible. The North corner is deep in the shower, between it bail wall and the wall the shower-head is on. The South and West are plainly visible, I'm glad to report.

The center, where you stand to do the LBR, is at the sharp point of the L. It is tempting to divide the place into 2 rooms and do ritual accordingly, but that leaves the bed area unprotected unless one stands on the bed. And totally closing it off with bookcases, while possible, and working in just the "living area", is quite incompatible with keeping the place livable (and it's rotten feng shui) because the place is so small. And the layout puts the altar against the west wall, as close to the bed area's NE corner as possible. Unless I want it in deep in the master closet between two rows of storage racks.

I do envy your Cthulhuverse polytheists who just set up an altar on the dresser and be done with it. Well, at least privacy is no issue, being on the 4th floor. Any magical advice beyond that most gratefully appreciated.
Pat, Keeping it simple in a small space

x

replies.title: Re: Magic in odd-shaped quarters
replies.content:
Don't worry about it. Seriously, don't worry about it. You don't have to face exactly toward the four directions; you don't have to banish the entire space -- remember that the ritual is primarily affecting your energy body, so wherever you go will be protected by your presence. Form the intention that the ritual will cleanse the entire apartment, and it will.

One of these days I'll write up a ritual that just involves setting up an altar on the dresser. It really can be that simple.

comments.title: Cats, Faeries, and the Earth Light
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer:

Thank you for these Magic Mondays, they are truly informative and I appreciate the conversations and questions raised here. I have a few questions of my own to ask.

1) I'm currently in a situation where I'm unable to remove my cats from my bedroom/meditation space. Normally they're well behaved and keep their distance, but having recently come back from a trip I've found them to be understandably "clingy" (with one in particular being aggresivly insistent that she sit on my lap during meditation and purr loud enough to put muscle cars to shame). While this is adorable and apreciated it's awefully distracting, the obvious solution is to find a way to temporarly evict them from the room (I'm working on that); but my concern is when I perform the Qabalistic Cross and Sphere of Light from Gareth Knight's Experience of the Inner Worlds, what effect might happen when my cats are in the same space with me during this operation?
2) My current magical studies and practice invloves the above mention book by Gareth Knight along with his Practical Guide to Qabalistic Symbolism and related works. Once I complete this line of practice (several years from now) I would like to next study a complementary system with a more Green Ray focus, such as the Celtic Faery tradition. I'm leaning towards R.J. Stewarts teachings on this tradition, do you have any positive or negative opinion on his work, if any? Needless to say, I take the "don't mix and match magical systems at once" approach to heart.
3)Piggy-backing off of the above question about Lucifer, I've noticed how in R.J. Stewart's books concerning Faery and the Underworld tradition he's quick to point out that the Light-Bringer and the Devil are not the same being, rather that the former is the Earth Light or star within our planet who none the less fell from a higher state, and talks about a future redemption of Lucifer via the Christ and a Threefold Allience of Human, Faery, and Animal. (My apologies if I'm mispresenting his teaching, I'm going by memory). I've seen a similar theme in Anthroposophy where the Christ being is needed to serve as balance between Lucifer and Ahriman so that the best of both can be fulfilled. A) What to you make of this esoteric Christian impulse to redeem Lucifer through Christ, and B) what connection to faeries have with fallen angels, if so why is working with them encouraged?

Thank you in advance for your time and consideration, I really apreciate it. I'm not sure if I'll be able to comment or respond again to day. Thank you, and many blessings to you and your loved ones.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Cats, Faeries, and the Earth Light
replies.content:
1) Cats adore magic, it's good for them, and they contribute power to the work -- that's why so many witches kept them back in the day. Back when we had a cat, whenever I'd set up an altar to do magic, she'd make a beeline to it, curl up under it, and go to sleep. Work on learning to meditate with a cat on your lap, and let them be part of your ritual space.

2) I worked my way through Stewart's Merlin work, and also practiced his UnderWorld Initiation extensively for a while. It's solid stuff and you'll learn a great deal from it.

3) The idea that the powers of evil can be redeemed goes way back, and it seems quite reasonable -- but remember tnat it's not you or I who does the redeeming! As for the other question, faeries are one kind of being and demons are quite different; confusing them is like confusing apples with orangutans.

comments.title: Pondering
comments.content:
1. I have been pondering why people are not recognized in their later incarnations. It stumps me that JMG would not be able to identify Dion Fortune in this current life. Though in the case of DF, whoever she is now probably got into magic pretty early in life, figured things out, and recognized the need to hide.

But that level of foresight surely doesn't apply to people who are not as spiritually wise. I cannot understand why an expert musician in this life, say Yo Yo Ma, wouldn't recognize Mozart. It just seems that such people, even in later incarnations, would be obvious to those in their field. What do you think?

2. Do you think Mozart was scrubbed by his Higher Self? This line of thinking came to me when you described a past life wherein you died unexpectedly in your thirties. Mozart seems like the type of person who may have reached the pinnacle of a craft without necessarily the spiritual development to move on.

What would such a subsequent incarnation look like? I imagined a boy monk in rural Thailand who spends his whole life dedicated to spirituality. He knows the most beautiful music runs through his head, but there's nothing he can do about it. Something like that?

Thank you for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: Re: Pondering
replies.content:
Okay, imagine you meet a 50 year old African American man who's a really gifted occultist. That man is the reincarnation of Dion Fortune. He doesn't have her personality, her tastes, or her writing or speaking style -- all that was shed with the lower vehicles -- and he doesn't practice the same kind of magic she does. You might guess, realizing that his skill at occultism is probably the work of many lives, that he was a famous occultist in his last incarnation -- but was he Fortune, Westcott, Yeats, George Winslow Plummer, Max Heindel, or any of the other people I've had as poster children on these Magic Monday posts? You would have no way of knowing.

In exactly the same way, when Mozart reincarnated, he will have had the same pyrotechnic musical talent, but it won't have been expressed in the same way unless he happened to be born a second time as the son of a classical musician who exposed him to the specific influences he got. Imagine that Mozart was reborn in Tibet in 1822. He'd have had the talent, but he'd have ended up composing and performing a completely different kind of music, because that's what he was exposed to as he grew up, became a monk, and studied the very rich traditions of Tibetan Buddhist ritual music.

replies.title: Re: Pondering
replies.content: If I may make it even worse: some of these occultists may even have been the same soul, and we'd have no way of knowing....

comments.content:
A few weeks ago, the topic of diabetes and etheric nutrition came up, and so I decided to do some research. I've decided to limit myself to type II diabetes, since type I looks like a classic auto-immune disorder. I think type II diabetes is caused by etheric starvation mixed with physical overeating, and here's the report outlining why.

One of the topics which came up a few times in diabetes research is "intra-cellular starvation", which seems to match the idea of etheric starvation. Additionally, type II diabetes was practically unknown until the 20th century. Prior to that, it would be remarkably hard to arrange to overeat and starve the etheric body at the same time, so this seems to support the connection.

A hallmark of the disease is insulin resistance, which is where the cells of the body begin to refuse to respond to insulin normally. Diabetes itself develops when the pancreas loses the ability to secrete sufficient insulin to overcome the cell's reluctance to respond.

All the symptoms unfold from the resulting hormonal and physiological issues, but at root the disease is insulin resistance. Insulin is a fairly interesting hormone: it serves a range of functions, but there are three which are important, and it's these three which are impaired in insulin resistance; in fact, part of the problem is that for many other purposes the body responds to insulin like normal, thus leading to excessive cellular potassium intake, and unusually intense arterial relaxation, among other issues.

The three purposes which cells develop a resistance are the absorption of glucose, the storage of fats and glucose, and a reduction in the rate of protein synthesis. These are interesting, because all three of these are adaptive responses to a lack of food. Glucose is the only source of fuel for the brain, and so during times when it's scare it's rationed for neurons only. When it's abundant, cells use it preferentially, but if it's scarce, since the brain can't use anything else, the rest of the body turns to other sources of fuel. If there's not enough glucose for the brain to use, then the liver has methods of producing more, which are activated in diabetes despite an excess of sugar already in the blood, causing further issues.

Fats are produced when food is abundant, and used as fuel later, when it's scarce. Insulin tells the body to hold onto fat, but with insulin resistance, fat cells will release them instead. Of course, they'll take the fuels the body has and make new ones even while this is happening, with the result being some unusual issues with fat cells.

The third one, the destruction of proteins, is a fairly drastic measure undertaken when the body is in full blown starvation mode. This is akin to tearing down the walls in order to have fuel to keep the fire burning: if you can't replace the walls before long, you run into problems. A certain number of proteins are taken apart constantly for a variety of reasons, but in a setting of insulin resistance a very large number are taken apart constantly. They're quickly rebuilt, but this produces a degree of metabolic stress.

So, at a cellular level, the body behaves as if starving, while at the same time the excess of glucose causes damage to the body's systems, such as the kidneys; at the same time, while fat cells are frantically releasing fuel for the body, they're drenched in material which needs to be processed, causing excessive fat production as well, leading to fairly drastic, rapid weight gain.

Finally, there's some evidence to suggest insulin resistance is caused by a lack of leptin. Leptin is a hormone that serves a couple purposes, but the primary one is signaling that you've had enough to eat. This seems plausible, but a leptin deficiency has another side effect: it drastically increases hunger. If, as seems likely, leptin deficiency occurs because the etheric body is hungry, the result will be overeating as the etheric body never gets the food it needs, and so leptin isn't produced, leading to constant hunger.

If there's anything anyone else can think of I should look into, or to add, I'd love to hear it.

comments.content:
Good evening JMG and all, I wonder if I can take up a bit of space describing an intriguing experience I had, in case any readers have experience or know lore that might help me understand.
This is a case of lights in the night that don't quite line up with any of the obvious explanations. Night before last I was visiting my sister in rural western Maine. Beautiful clear night, big orange moon. Sometime as I was falling asleep I was startled by a sharp noise right outside the house. I went to the window and listened (even with the moon it was too dark to see much). The sound repeated every so often and after a few minutes I was pretty sure what I was hearing was a big deer snorting and stomping in the vegetable garden. And that's what it was, based on the evidence we discovered in the morning. (So much for the cabbages.)
But as I was sitting by the window listening in the dark, I realized I was seeing lights moving through the air. At first I supposed they were fireflies whose lights were being smudged by the screen or the reflection in the window, but they weren't at all like the lights of fireflies, which are small, bright points of light pulsing on periodically. These were larger, maybe the size of a bird, and were sort of a fainter flickering glow, without a definite shape. They were a pale white-yellow, maybe a bit greenish (and I kept thinking I was seeing reddish ones but could never get my eyes on one). A light would glow and flicker for a second, then disappear for a beat, then flicker back on for a second, so that I could follow the path of an individual light for two or three seconds before it went dark or I lost it. Their speed and movement made me wonder if they was some type of large, bioluminescent moth. They flew over the large open space of the garden, in the woods around it, and down by the road fifty yards below, which was the limit of my sight. There were dozens of them appearing and disappearing; at any given instant I was seeing four or five of them. This lasted for ten minutes as I sat watching them and periodically hearing the deer, and hadn't stopped when I went back to bed. (Shame on me but I didn't scare the deer out of the garden; my kids were asleep in the room, and the deer have had a tough season.)
My brother-in-law has lived all his life on that hill and couldn't say what it was. We googled a bit and nothing made much sense. What came to mind as I watched them was fairies, though in more of the Tinkerbell image than what I know of faery lore. Some sort of nature spirit seems plausible, though; this is a very rural area in the forest without plumbing or electricity and a garden that my sister has cultivated very considerately over decades. She's said before that there are definitely some faery or other kind of beings present there.
I hate to take up so much space with something that's of interest only to some, so thanks for indulging me. If anyone in northern New England or elsewhere has notes to compare, I'm interested. Thank you JMG, as always, for this forum.
Jonathan.
